category.name: Red Flags Reference
category.summary: Early warning signals in code, teams, process, leadership, and AI usage that suggest deeper problems may already exist or are forming.

entries.title: Nobody can explain this module simply
entries.summary: A module performs important work, but nobody can describe its purpose in plain language without hand-waving.
entries.decisionHeuristic: If a module cannot be explained simply, its boundary is probably already decaying.
entries.whatYouNotice: Explanations become circular, jargon-heavy, or depend on historical trivia rather than current purpose.
entries.whyItMatters: Poor conceptual clarity usually means poor ownership, weak boundaries, and dangerous change behavior.
whatItUsuallyIndicates: knowledge concentrated in a few people
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: the module is genuinely low-level and highly specialized, but at least one owner can still explain it clearly
the audience lacks domain context but documentation and ownership are strong
leadingIndicators: different people describe the module differently
ownership boundaries sound vague
bug fixes in the module require multiple experts
diagnosticQuestions: What single job does this module do?
What would clearly not belong here?
Who is accountable for its behavior?
antiPatterns: adding more generic naming instead of clarifying boundaries
treating confusion as a documentation-only problem
falseFriends: it is complex so nobody can explain it simply
the code works so the conceptual model must be fine
aiAmplifies: AI can generate confident explanations that sound coherent while preserving underlying architectural confusion.
aiMasks: AI summaries can make the module sound cleaner than it really is.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Generated documentation creates false confidence in a boundary nobody has actually cleaned up.
exampleSignals: It handles orchestration, transformation, policy, and fallback logic.
Ask Sam, they know how it really works.
We should not touch that right now.

entries.title: One file does too much
entries.summary: A single file accumulates unrelated responsibilities and becomes a local gravity well.
entries.decisionHeuristic: When one file becomes the easiest place to add everything, you are already paying for missing structure.
entries.whatYouNotice: Large files mixing business logic, validation, orchestration, transformations, and edge-case handling.
entries.whyItMatters: Local convenience becomes systemic fragility. The file becomes hard to understand, review, and safely change.
whatItUsuallyIndicates: urgent patches landing in the easiest visible place
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: the file is large because of data tables or declarative mappings that remain easy to reason about
a code generator owns the file and it is not manually edited
leadingIndicators: reviewers skip full-file review
small changes trigger fear
merge conflicts cluster in one place
diagnosticQuestions: How many distinct reasons does this file change?
Could this file be explained as one coherent concept?
What responsibilities are accidentally coupled here?
antiPatterns: solving file growth by adding regions or comments instead of splitting responsibilities
treating line count as the only signal
falseFriends: big file equals bad design in all cases
small files automatically mean good design
aiAmplifies: AI makes it easy to append more logic into the already-central file because it predicts the local pattern well.
aiMasks: Uniform generated style can make a bloated file look orderly.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: AI keeps extending the same giant file because it is the highest-context place in the repository.
exampleSignals: This file is where the real behavior lives.
Do not split it yet or we will lose the flow.
Every incident fix touches this file.

entries.title: Changes always touch too many places
entries.summary: Even ordinary changes require edits across many files, layers, or services.
entries.decisionHeuristic: When routine changes fan out widely, the system is telling you its boundaries are wrong or too coupled.
entries.whatYouNotice: Simple requests require multiple coordinated changes across layers that should have been more local.
entries.whyItMatters: Broad change surface increases risk, slows delivery, and makes ownership murky.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: the change is genuinely cross-cutting by nature
a one-time migration is in progress and is explicitly acknowledged
leadingIndicators: ordinary features get framed as system-wide work
review size grows faster than value delivered
teams ask for coordination meetings before small changes
diagnosticQuestions: Why is this change not local?
Which dependency makes the fan-out unavoidable?
Are we paying for technical coupling or organizational coupling?
antiPatterns: celebrating cross-team coordination as proof of maturity when it is often compensating for bad structure
accepting fan-out as normal
falseFriends: complex business domain automatically requires broad code fan-out
aiAmplifies: AI can speed up multi-place edits, reducing friction while preserving the underlying architectural problem.
aiMasks: Generated cross-repo changes can make fan-out feel operationally normal.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Teams mistake faster fan-out editing for healthier boundaries.
exampleSignals: It is just a small change, but it touches six services.
We need three teams for this simple rule update.

entries.title: Naming is generic where understanding is weak
entries.summary: As conceptual clarity drops, code and design names become broader and more vague.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Generic naming is often a symptom, not the disease. Follow it to the unclear boundary underneath.
entries.whatYouNotice: Names like Manager, Processor, Util, Handler, Common, Core, Engine appear where domain meaning should be stronger.
entries.whyItMatters: Weak naming hides weak modeling. It reduces shared understanding and makes refactoring harder.
whatItUsuallyIndicates: fear of committing to a real model
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: the abstraction is intentionally generic and tightly scoped
the name is stable because the role is infrastructural and well understood
leadingIndicators: different files use the same vague nouns for different jobs
code reviews rarely challenge names
domain experts do not recognize terms used in code
diagnosticQuestions: Could a non-author explain what this component is for?
Is this name hiding multiple responsibilities?
Does the domain have better words than the code does?
whatToCheckNext: domain language in product or business docs
change reasons for the generic component
antiPatterns: renaming without changing structure
using generic nouns as a substitute for design clarity
falseFriends: good naming alone fixes bad boundaries
aiAmplifies: AI tends to generate safe generic names when the model lacks strong domain signals.
aiMasks: Consistent generic naming can look orderly and intentional.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Generated code spreads bland naming conventions faster than the team notices conceptual erosion.
exampleSignals: We have DataManager, SyncManager, RuleEngine, and CommonProcessor.
It is generic because we might reuse it later.

entries.title: Business logic leaks across layers
entries.summary: Rules and decisions show up in controllers, mappers, jobs, UI, SQL, and integrations instead of one clear home.
entries.decisionHeuristic: When business logic appears everywhere, it effectively has no owner.
entries.whatYouNotice: The same rule is partially duplicated in multiple runtime or architectural layers.
entries.whyItMatters: Behavior becomes hard to trust, hard to test, and hard to change consistently.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: light validation is intentionally duplicated near boundaries for safety and the canonical logic is still clear
leadingIndicators: rule changes need coordinated edits across unrelated layers
bugs appear as inconsistent behavior rather than hard failures
diagnosticQuestions: Where does this rule truly live?
Which layer is canonical when behavior disagrees?
Are we validating the same concept or redefining it in each layer?
whatToCheckNext: duplicate behavior across UI, API, and persistence
antiPatterns: fixing the visible instance of the rule without tracing all copies
calling boundary validation a domain model
falseFriends: every repeated check is bad duplication
aiAmplifies: AI often continues the nearest local pattern, spreading copied business rules across layers faster.
aiMasks: Generated consistency in syntax hides inconsistency in actual source of truth.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: AI-assisted teams copy rules into adapters and UI surfaces because it is locally convenient.
exampleSignals: The UI validates it one way, the API another, and the batch job a third.
We need to update this rule in three repositories.

entries.title: Tests are hard to write for normal changes
entries.summary: Ordinary work feels harder to test than it should, even when the change itself is not unusual.
entries.decisionHeuristic: If normal changes are painful to test, your design is already taxing delivery.
entries.whatYouNotice: Developers avoid adding tests, require huge setup, or rely on manual verification for routine changes.
entries.whyItMatters: Poor testability is usually a proxy for poor design, excessive coupling, or hidden runtime assumptions.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: you are testing an unusually integration-heavy legacy surface during an explicitly temporary transition
leadingIndicators: test setup is larger than the change
developers skip tests for speed
the team depends heavily on QA or staging confidence
diagnosticQuestions: What makes this change hard to test?
Is the pain from tooling, or from structure?
Where are the missing seams?
antiPatterns: accepting low testability as normal for the codebase
adding only high-level tests to compensate for poor design
falseFriends: testing is just always expensive in serious systems
aiAmplifies: AI can generate tests that compile but do not reduce the real pain of a poorly testable structure.
aiMasks: Test count rises while true confidence does not.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Teams use AI to generate brittle tests around bad design instead of improving the design.
exampleSignals: This is a small fix, but I cannot write a sane test for it.
We will validate this manually in staging.

entries.title: End-to-end tests carry all the confidence
entries.summary: The team relies mainly on slow, broad tests because lower-level confidence is weak or absent.
entries.decisionHeuristic: If end-to-end tests are your only safety net, they are probably covering for missing design and test seams.
entries.whatYouNotice: Pipeline trust depends mostly on large system tests while unit, component, or contract tests are thin or untrusted.
entries.whyItMatters: Confidence becomes slow, expensive, flaky, and difficult to localize.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: a small system is intentionally tested mainly through a few stable end-to-end flows and this remains affordable
leadingIndicators: long pipelines
flake anxiety
developers cannot tell where a failure originates
diagnosticQuestions: What lower-level confidence is missing?
Which key behaviors could be proven earlier and cheaper?
Do end-to-end tests verify product reality or substitute for missing seams?
commonRootCauses: design not testable in smaller scopes
antiPatterns: solving all quality concerns by adding more end-to-end tests
treating pipeline duration as the unavoidable cost of confidence
falseFriends: end-to-end tests are more real, so they should dominate
aiAmplifies: AI can generate more broad tests quickly, increasing volume without fixing confidence distribution.
aiMasks: Large test suites can look like maturity while actually signaling design weakness.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: AI-generated end-to-end tests inflate coverage dashboards while lower-level trust remains weak.
exampleSignals: If the browser suite passes, we ship.
We do not really trust the unit tests.

entries.title: Shared utility layer grows faster than products
entries.summary: The common or shared layer expands aggressively while real product or domain code remains less stable or less clear.
entries.decisionHeuristic: When shared code grows faster than concrete needs, abstraction is probably outrunning reality.
entries.whatYouNotice: Common, shared, platform, core, or util packages grow rapidly and become the default destination for new logic.
entries.whyItMatters: Shared layers often become dumping grounds that increase coupling and reduce local clarity.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: a proven platform capability is being intentionally built for multiple active consumers
leadingIndicators: teams add to shared layers before local implementation proves the pattern
common code has many optional paths and flags
diagnosticQuestions: How many real consumers does this shared layer have?
Would this code be clearer if it stayed local longer?
Is reuse proven or merely hoped for?
antiPatterns: moving local code into shared space after one use
treating shared code volume as leverage
falseFriends: more shared code means more maturity
aiAmplifies: AI makes packaging reusable-looking helpers cheap, increasing shared-layer sprawl.
aiMasks: Generated abstractions look clean and coherent enough to evade skepticism.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: AI-assisted teams extract shared helpers before the second real consumer exists.
exampleSignals: Let us put it in common so other teams can use it later.
Most of our changes are in shared-core now.

entries.title: Integration contracts are implicit
entries.summary: Systems depend on each other through assumptions that are not clearly documented, versioned, or enforced.
entries.decisionHeuristic: If the contract only lives in working code and tribal memory, it is not stable enough.
entries.whatYouNotice: Consumers and producers rely on behavior that is assumed, inferred, or learned through incidents rather than explicit agreements.
entries.whyItMatters: Implicit contracts break silently, make upgrades risky, and create expensive coordination failures.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: a short-lived internal prototype is intentionally informal and low-risk
leadingIndicators: breaking changes are found late
consumer teams discover assumptions through production behavior
versioning is ad hoc
diagnosticQuestions: What exactly is guaranteed here?
Which behaviors are consumers relying on informally?
How would a new team know the contract?
antiPatterns: calling it internal so it does not need a contract
assuming tests in one repo prove a contract across repos
falseFriends: it works now, so the contract is effectively clear
aiAmplifies: AI can generate consumers quickly against inferred contracts, increasing hidden dependence on unstated behavior.
aiMasks: Generated client code makes implicit contracts feel official.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: AI-assisted consumers lock onto examples rather than guarantees.
exampleSignals: Do not change that field, another team might rely on it.
We never documented that, but yes, consumers expect it.

entries.title: Generated code is merged without deep review
entries.summary: Code enters production mainly because it looks plausible, not because reviewers truly understand it.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Generated code still needs accountable human understanding, not just approval.
entries.whatYouNotice: Review speed rises, diff size grows, and explanations of the change get thinner.
entries.whyItMatters: AI can accelerate delivery and accelerate misunderstanding at the same time.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: the generated output is trivial, low-risk, and bounded by strong automated checks
leadingIndicators: reviewers ask fewer conceptual questions
authors cannot explain major sections of the diff
code style looks clean but design intent is fuzzy
diagnosticQuestions: Who truly understands this change?
Could the author explain the failure modes of this code?
What verification exists beyond readability?
commonRootCauses: insufficient review norms for AI-assisted work
antiPatterns: merging because the code compiles and tests pass
treating generated code as lower-effort review because the style looks consistent
falseFriends: clean syntax means the design is sound
passing tests prove the author understood the code
aiAmplifies: This is itself an AI-amplified red flag.
aiMasks: Uniform style and fluent comments make weakly understood code feel trustworthy.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Review becomes approval-shaped because the generator already did the hard-looking part.
exampleSignals: Looks good to me.
The AI generated most of it but tests are green.
I have not read every path, but it seems fine.

entries.title: Everyone asks the same person
entries.summary: One person becomes the default source of truth, escalation path, or decision gateway for too many important areas.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Repeated dependence on one person is not efficiency; it is usually concentrated risk.
entries.whatYouNotice: Questions, approvals, incidents, design clarification, and tribal knowledge all converge on the same individual.
entries.whyItMatters: The team becomes fragile, slower, and more dependent on personal availability than shared capability.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: a temporary deep-dive subject matter expert is assisting during a short-lived transition and transfer is already planned
leadingIndicators: people wait for one person's approval
vacations slow delivery noticeably
questions route to the same individual regardless of domain
diagnosticQuestions: What knowledge or authority is concentrated here?
Would the team still function well if this person were unavailable for two weeks?
Is the dependency technical, social, or managerial?
antiPatterns: celebrating indispensability
treating individual heroics as evidence of strong culture
falseFriends: they are just very experienced
it is faster to ask them
aiAmplifies: AI can reduce obvious knowledge bottlenecks in some areas, but can also deepen the dependency if only one person knows how to use the tools safely.
aiMasks: AI summaries can make expertise look more distributed than it is.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: One person becomes the AI whisperer everyone depends on for prompt, tooling, and policy interpretation.
exampleSignals: Ask Alex.
They are the only one who really knows that part.
We cannot deploy until they look at it.

entries.title: Critical knowledge lives in chat and memory
entries.summary: Important operational or architectural knowledge exists mainly in people's heads or scattered chat history.
entries.decisionHeuristic: If the team cannot recover a decision without asking a person, the team is depending on memory instead of systems.
entries.whatYouNotice: Questions are answered from memory, and prior decisions are rediscovered through private history rather than shared artifacts.
entries.whyItMatters: Institutional memory becomes fragile, onboarding slows, and incidents become harder to resolve.
whatItUsuallyIndicates: high delivery pressure crowding out knowledge capture
culture that values speed over durable memory
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: the topic is genuinely ephemeral and low-risk
leadingIndicators: onboarding depends heavily on shadowing
same questions recur
people search chat before looking for docs because docs are not trusted
diagnosticQuestions: What decisions would we lose if key people left?
Which operational steps are recoverable without direct human recall?
Are docs missing, stale, or ignored?
antiPatterns: assuming chat history is good enough as system memory
writing docs after every crisis and never maintaining them
falseFriends: our team is small, so we do not need durable memory
aiAmplifies: AI can summarize chat history, which helps temporarily, but also reduces pressure to create authoritative shared artifacts.
aiMasks: Summaries can make fragmented memory look organized without fixing source truth.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Teams ask AI for context instead of repairing the actual knowledge system.
exampleSignals: I think we decided that in Slack.
It is somewhere in Teams.
I remember why we did this, but it is not written down.

entries.title: People avoid touching certain areas
entries.summary: Parts of the system become socially dangerous, so engineers avoid them unless forced.
entries.decisionHeuristic: When fear shapes code ownership, the system is already signaling design or knowledge debt.
entries.whatYouNotice: Certain modules, services, or workflows are approached reluctantly and only by a small subset of the team.
entries.whyItMatters: Avoidance reduces shared ownership, increases risk concentration, and turns ordinary changes into escalations.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: the area is intentionally restricted for high-risk operational reasons and ownership is still healthy
leadingIndicators: people warn others away from an area
refactors are postponed repeatedly
only one or two people volunteer for changes there
diagnosticQuestions: What are people afraid of exactly?
Is the fear about complexity, blast radius, or missing knowledge?
What would make the area safer to touch?
antiPatterns: declaring an area scary instead of improving it
routing all risk to the same expert
falseFriends: respect for complexity automatically means healthy caution
aiAmplifies: AI can make people feel bolder temporarily, but without understanding it can also increase risky edits in feared areas.
aiMasks: AI help can hide how weak real ownership still is.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Teams use AI to touch feared code without improving understanding or safety nets.
exampleSignals: Nobody wants to touch billing.
Only Chris edits that part.
Let us not risk it before release.

entries.title: PRs are approved faster than they are understood
entries.summary: Review speed outruns review depth, so approvals become a workflow ritual rather than a quality mechanism.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Fast approval is only healthy if understanding remains visible and real.
entries.whatYouNotice: Large or subtle changes get approved quickly with low-substance comments or only superficial review.
entries.whyItMatters: Review stops serving learning, safety, and design scrutiny, especially under AI-assisted development.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: the change is tiny, low-risk, and well-covered by strong automation
leadingIndicators: LGTM dominates review culture
review comments rarely question design intent
review duration drops while change size rises
diagnosticQuestions: What evidence shows the reviewer understood the change?
Are reviewers overloaded or disengaged?
What types of changes are slipping through shallow review?
whatToCheckNext: post-merge incidents tied to reviewed code
antiPatterns: measuring review health by speed alone
assuming tests replace conceptual review
falseFriends: fast review equals good team velocity
clean-looking code needs less review
aiAmplifies: AI expands diff size and apparent fluency, making shallow review even more dangerous.
aiMasks: Generated code style reduces visual signals that something is conceptually wrong.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Review becomes approval-shaped because generated code looks orderly and complete.
exampleSignals: Looks good.
Approved, did not read every path.
Green checks, so I merged it.

entries.title: Ownership is claimed but not visible
entries.summary: People say an area is owned, but ownership is not observable in decisions, maintenance, documentation, or response patterns.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Real ownership should be visible in behavior, not just in org charts or service catalogs.
entries.whatYouNotice: Named owners exist, but issue triage, roadmap choices, docs, and incident response do not reflect clear stewardship.
entries.whyItMatters: Invisible ownership creates diffuse accountability and slow action when things go wrong.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: ownership transfer is actively in progress and explicitly communicated
leadingIndicators: owners are unclear during incidents
documentation has no accountable maintainer
backlog and roadmap decisions are made elsewhere
diagnosticQuestions: What decisions can the owner actually make?
What maintenance signals show stewardship?
Who updates docs, runbooks, and dependencies here?
antiPatterns: assigning ownership in tooling without stewardship expectations
using 'owned' as a label rather than an operational reality
falseFriends: it is in the service catalog, so it must be owned
aiAmplifies: AI can make routine maintenance tasks easier, which may temporarily hide weak ownership until higher-stakes decisions are needed.
aiMasks: Generated docs and summaries can make stewardship look healthier than it is.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: AI-maintained artifacts stay current while real decision ownership remains unclear.
exampleSignals: Team X owns it, but they need approval from three other groups.
There is an owner, but nobody knows who updates the runbook.

entries.title: No one disagrees in meetings, everyone complains later
entries.summary: Visible consensus in formal settings hides real disagreement that emerges only afterward in side channels.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Silence in decision rooms is not alignment unless concerns are truly surfaced and resolved.
entries.whatYouNotice: Meetings appear smooth, but objections surface later in private chats, hallway conversations, or passive resistance.
entries.whyItMatters: Fake alignment produces poor decisions, weak commitment, and repeated reopening of issues.
whatItUsuallyIndicates: meeting norms that reward smoothness over truth
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: the topic was already debated deeply beforehand and the meeting is only formal closure
leadingIndicators: side conversations are more candid than formal ones
decisions get quietly undermined later
same concerns recur after 'alignment'
diagnosticQuestions: Where does real disagreement surface?
Who does not feel safe to disagree in the room?
Do meetings reward honesty or smooth closure?
antiPatterns: treating visible calm as proof of alignment
penalizing dissent implicitly
falseFriends: we are aligned because nobody objected
aiAmplifies: AI meeting summaries can flatten disagreement and make tension appear resolved.
aiMasks: Polished summaries can erase nuance and unresolved objections.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Decision records reflect summary consensus while real disagreement remains off-record.
exampleSignals: No objections? Great, we are aligned.
After the meeting, three people said they never agreed.

entries.title: The loudest person wins architecture discussions
entries.summary: Architecture outcomes follow assertiveness, seniority, or force of personality more than evidence and context.
entries.decisionHeuristic: If the decision quality tracks status more than evidence, the room is already biased.
entries.whatYouNotice: Certain people dominate design outcomes regardless of preparation quality, domain fit, or opposing evidence.
entries.whyItMatters: Decision quality degrades, dissent goes underground, and design becomes political rather than empirical.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: a strong opinion is also the strongest evidence-based position and the process still allows real challenge
leadingIndicators: the same people always frame the choice space
counterarguments are dismissed quickly
decisions align with authority gradients
diagnosticQuestions: What evidence changed the decision?
Would the outcome be different if names were removed?
Who speaks least and why?
antiPatterns: confusing confidence with correctness
using seniority as a shortcut for decision quality
falseFriends: strong opinions from experts are always the most efficient way to decide
aiAmplifies: AI-generated decks and summaries can arm dominant voices with polished but weakly grounded arguments.
aiMasks: High-quality presentation can make shaky reasoning look rigorous.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: The winning argument is the best AI-polished one, not the best evidenced one.
exampleSignals: We are doing this because leadership likes the direction.
Nobody challenged the strongest voice in the room.

entries.title: AI use is widespread but norms are unclear
entries.summary: People use AI heavily, but the team lacks shared rules about where it is safe, expected, or dangerous.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Broad tool usage without shared norms creates invisible quality variance.
entries.whatYouNotice: Some people use AI constantly, some avoid it, and nobody can clearly explain the team’s expectations.
entries.whyItMatters: Quality, authorship, review depth, and risk exposure start to vary by individual habit rather than team design.
whatItUsuallyIndicates: social rather than policy-based tool norms
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: the team is in an explicit short experimentation phase and is actively turning observations into norms
leadingIndicators: different reviewers expect different standards
AI use is disclosed inconsistently
mistakes reveal uneven assumptions about safe use
diagnosticQuestions: Where do we allow AI use and why?
What work still requires direct human authorship or deeper review?
How do we review AI-assisted changes differently?
whatToCheckNext: incident examples tied to AI usage
antiPatterns: letting each engineer invent their own AI safety model
pretending everyone is already aligned because usage is common
falseFriends: tool ubiquity means healthy adoption maturity
aiAmplifies: This red flag is itself AI-driven.
aiMasks: High output and clean style can make uneven usage quality harder to notice.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Different team members operate under incompatible assumptions about AI disclosure, verification, and acceptable use.
exampleSignals: Everyone uses it differently.
I did not know we were supposed to disclose AI-generated sections.
Our review bar depends on who wrote it.

entries.title: Work enters faster than it leaves
entries.summary: Incoming work volume consistently outpaces completion, so queues, context switching, and churn grow silently.
entries.decisionHeuristic: If intake exceeds throughput for long enough, the system is already degrading even if dashboards still look busy.
entries.whatYouNotice: Backlogs expand, WIP grows, priorities churn, and teams feel busy without feeling done.
entries.whyItMatters: Unbounded intake creates hidden delay, quality erosion, and loss of focus.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: a deliberate short-term surge is being buffered and explicitly managed
leadingIndicators: backlog grows despite strong effort
people carry too much WIP
interruptions routinely displace in-flight work
diagnosticQuestions: Who controls intake?
Which work is entering without displacing other work?
Are we underestimating how long work truly stays open?
antiPatterns: adding capacity targets without reducing intake
treating backlog growth as normal demand capture
falseFriends: a bigger backlog means stronger demand and therefore health
aiAmplifies: AI can make work creation easier than work completion, increasing ticket and artifact inflow.
aiMasks: Productivity-looking output can hide queue expansion.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: AI speeds specification and ticket generation without improving the system’s actual exit rate.
exampleSignals: We finished a lot, but the board still looks worse.
We keep adding work faster than we can close it.

entries.title: Everything is urgent
entries.summary: Priority loses meaning because too many items are treated as immediate and exceptional at the same time.
entries.decisionHeuristic: When everything is urgent, nothing is prioritized-only interrupted.
entries.whatYouNotice: Teams switch rapidly, deadlines multiply, and ordinary planning cannot survive incoming escalation.
entries.whyItMatters: Constant urgency destroys focus, hides trade-offs, and corrodes delivery predictability.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: a real incident window or crisis period is explicitly declared and time-bounded
leadingIndicators: priority labels lose meaning
planned work is routinely displaced
people stop trusting sprint or plan commitments
diagnosticQuestions: Who benefits from calling work urgent?
What criteria distinguish urgent from important?
What work is suffering because urgency is inflated?
antiPatterns: solving urgency overload with better status reporting alone
using urgency language as a negotiation tool
falseFriends: high urgency means the team is working on important things
aiAmplifies: AI can accelerate escalation artifacts, making it even easier to create apparently urgent work quickly.
aiMasks: Fast summaries can make interruption patterns look well-managed rather than pathological.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: AI-generated briefs increase the number of polished 'urgent' asks without improving prioritization quality.
exampleSignals: Can you just do this quickly today?
Everything is P0 this quarter.

entries.title: Scope changes without decision records
entries.summary: Meaningful scope shifts happen, but nobody captures who decided, why, or what trade-off was accepted.
entries.decisionHeuristic: If the scope changed and nobody can reconstruct the decision, the risk has been displaced, not managed.
entries.whatYouNotice: Features expand, shrink, or morph through chat, meetings, or habit rather than explicit recorded decisions.
entries.whyItMatters: Teams lose traceability for trade-offs, which makes conflict, rework, and blame more likely later.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: the change is genuinely trivial and local
leadingIndicators: people disagree later about what was agreed
scope loss or expansion surprises stakeholders
the team cannot explain why something was cut
diagnosticQuestions: Who changed the scope?
What constraint forced the trade-off?
Where is that decision visible to the team?
antiPatterns: using ticket edits as a substitute for decision records
assuming everyone remembers why the scope moved
falseFriends: moving scope quickly means the team is agile
aiAmplifies: AI summaries can compress scope discussions into neat outputs while dropping important trade-off nuance.
aiMasks: Generated meeting notes can look complete without capturing real decisions.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: AI-generated recap says 'aligned on revised scope' when the actual trade-offs were never made explicit.
exampleSignals: I thought that was out of scope now.
We agreed to cut that, but I cannot find where.

entries.title: Dates are fixed but trade-offs are implicit
entries.summary: A delivery date is treated as immovable, but the corresponding trade-offs in scope, quality, or risk are not stated openly.
entries.decisionHeuristic: A fixed date without explicit flexibility elsewhere is usually just concealed risk.
entries.whatYouNotice: Teams speak as if the date is certain, but nobody says what becomes flexible to preserve it.
entries.whyItMatters: Invisible trade-offs do not disappear; they move into quality, scope ambiguity, or team stress.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: the constraints are genuinely documented and accepted even if the date is externally fixed
leadingIndicators: quality language gets vaguer as the date approaches
scope quietly changes
teams say we will make it work without naming how
diagnosticQuestions: What exactly is flexible if the date is not?
Who approved the trade-off?
What risk is being priced into the plan?
antiPatterns: talking about confidence without naming trade-offs
letting quality absorb the schedule truth silently
falseFriends: confidence language means the plan is credible
aiAmplifies: AI can help produce polished status reporting that conceals unresolved trade-offs.
aiMasks: Summaries and dashboards can preserve the fiction of certainty longer.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: AI-generated plans become more articulate while remaining under-specified on what is actually flexible.
exampleSignals: The date is fixed, we will figure out the rest.
We are still on track, but details are evolving.

entries.title: Tickets substitute for thinking
entries.summary: The organization mistakes ticket flow for real design clarity, prioritization, and problem understanding.
entries.decisionHeuristic: A well-structured backlog cannot substitute for clear thinking about the problem.
entries.whatYouNotice: Work gets decomposed into many tickets, but important assumptions, trade-offs, and system consequences remain unclear.
entries.whyItMatters: Process visibility increases while actual shared understanding stays thin.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: tickets are deliberately lightweight pointers to already well-understood work
leadingIndicators: ticket count grows faster than clarity
teams discover key assumptions during implementation
stakeholders trust ticket structure more than technical reasoning
diagnosticQuestions: Does the ticket capture a solved problem or just a task shell?
What assumptions are still unstated?
What decision is this ticket pretending not to contain?
antiPatterns: splitting unclear work into smaller unclear work
measuring preparedness by backlog granularity
falseFriends: everything is in Jira so everyone is aligned
aiAmplifies: AI can generate neat tickets and acceptance criteria rapidly, increasing the illusion of readiness.
aiMasks: Polished ticket text makes underthinking harder to notice.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: AI-created tickets look complete while hiding that the problem framing is still weak.
exampleSignals: We have all the tickets, so we are ready.
Let us just create subtasks for the unknowns.

entries.title: Metrics are visible but not trusted
entries.summary: Dashboards, KPIs, and delivery metrics exist, but people do not believe they reflect reality well enough to act on them confidently.
entries.decisionHeuristic: If no one trusts the metric, the visibility is cosmetic.
entries.whatYouNotice: Teams still ask people for reality even though dashboards exist, or interpret dashboards defensively instead of operationally.
entries.whyItMatters: Measurement without trust creates reporting theater rather than better decisions.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: a new metric is still being calibrated openly and trust-building is active
leadingIndicators: teams caveat every dashboard discussion
manual spreadsheets or side channels are used to explain the truth
leaders ask for alternate numbers
diagnosticQuestions: What reality does this metric fail to capture?
Who trusts it least, and why?
What behavior is the metric incentivizing?
antiPatterns: adding more dashboards instead of repairing trust
using disputed metrics for judgment-heavy performance conversations
falseFriends: if it is measured, it is under control
aiAmplifies: AI can make reporting easier to produce, which can increase the volume of low-trust metrics quickly.
aiMasks: AI-generated narrative around a dashboard can make weak metrics feel more legitimate.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Evaluation dashboards look rigorous while task-ground truth remains thin.
exampleSignals: The dashboard says green, but everyone knows we are not green.
We do not use that metric for real decisions.

entries.title: Retrospectives repeat the same outputs
entries.summary: Teams keep naming the same problems in retros, but little structural change follows.
entries.decisionHeuristic: If retros only re-diagnose the same pain, they are becoming emotional ventilation instead of improvement.
entries.whatYouNotice: Themes repeat quarter after quarter: communication, dependencies, too much work, unclear priorities.
entries.whyItMatters: The team starts to lose faith that reflection changes reality.
whatItUsuallyIndicates: lack of authority to address root causes
too many local actions for systemic problems
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: the problem is genuinely systemic and the team is actively escalating it with evidence
leadingIndicators: same sticky notes every cycle
actions are small but causes are structural
team cynicism rises
diagnosticQuestions: What changed after the last three retros?
Are we naming a team problem or an organizational one?
Does the team have the authority to act on the real cause?
antiPatterns: assigning small local actions to structural constraints
running retros without reviewing previous action outcomes
falseFriends: the team is reflective because the same issues keep surfacing
aiAmplifies: AI can generate polished retro summaries and action items that create the feeling of progress without changing underlying authority or structure.
aiMasks: Well-written retro artifacts can hide repeated non-action.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: AI keeps suggesting similar generic actions while the real blocker remains systemic.
exampleSignals: We said the same thing last sprint.
Action: improve communication.

entries.title: Release confidence depends on luck and timing
entries.summary: Teams ship when the stars align rather than because the release process gives genuine confidence.
entries.decisionHeuristic: If confidence depends on timing, people, or low traffic windows more than repeatable controls, release safety is weak.
entries.whatYouNotice: Releases feel risky unless done at special times, with special people, or after manual checks and rituals.
entries.whyItMatters: Release quality becomes personality- and circumstance-dependent instead of system-dependent.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: a deliberately unusual high-risk migration is happening with exceptional controls
leadingIndicators: release windows are chosen around fear rather than policy
manual checklists dominate
rollback confidence is weak
diagnosticQuestions: Why do we feel safe shipping at this time but not another?
What part of confidence is automation, and what part is ritual?
Can we roll back fast and safely?
antiPatterns: normalizing special release rituals
trusting release folklore more than system design
falseFriends: our careful rituals prove we are disciplined
aiAmplifies: AI can generate release checklists and summaries, but cannot replace weak rollback or observability foundations.
aiMasks: Operational polish in release notes can hide unreliable release mechanics.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: AI-generated release coordination looks smooth while underlying deployment confidence remains shaky.
exampleSignals: We should deploy only when Dana is online.
If traffic is low enough, it is usually okay.

entries.title: Dependencies are discovered late every cycle
entries.summary: Important cross-team or cross-system dependencies are found after work is already underway, not during planning or framing.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Repeated late dependency discovery usually means the system of work is under-mapped, not just unlucky.
entries.whatYouNotice: Teams start work confidently, then uncover blockers, approvals, or integration needs late in execution.
entries.whyItMatters: Late dependency discovery ruins predictability and creates avoidable re-planning.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: a genuinely novel initiative is exploring unknown terrain and the uncertainty is explicit
leadingIndicators: planning confidence is high until implementation starts
blocked states appear late
cross-team meetings multiply mid-cycle
diagnosticQuestions: What did planning fail to reveal?
Are dependencies hidden technically, organizationally, or both?
Who knows the dependency graph best?
antiPatterns: treating every late dependency as a one-off surprise
planning detailed tasks without mapping the dependency surface
falseFriends: this always happens in complex environments
aiAmplifies: AI can speed up plan creation without improving actual dependency discovery.
aiMasks: Generated plans may look complete even when dependency mapping is shallow.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: AI-generated delivery plans omit hidden team and system dependencies because they are not well represented in the source material.
exampleSignals: We did not realize we needed platform support.
That dependency was only discovered during testing.

entries.title: Teams cannot explain what done means
entries.summary: Completion criteria are vague enough that teams, stakeholders, and reviewers mean different things by 'done.'
entries.decisionHeuristic: If done is fuzzy, delivery confidence is performative.
entries.whatYouNotice: Work moves through states, but completion criteria are inconsistent, local, or only implicit.
entries.whyItMatters: Vague done definitions create hidden rework, surprise quality gaps, and endless near-completion.
whatItUsuallyIndicates: quality and operations not integrated into delivery definition
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: exploratory discovery work is explicitly marked as learning rather than delivery
leadingIndicators: items appear done then reopen
handoffs reveal missing assumptions
teams talk about development done, QA done, release done separately without shared definition
diagnosticQuestions: What must be true for this to count as done?
Does done include rollout, observability, docs, and support readiness?
Who would disagree with our definition of done?
antiPatterns: tracking status with multiple incompatible done states
calling code-complete done
falseFriends: everyone knows what done means
aiAmplifies: AI can write polished acceptance criteria that still avoid the real operational meaning of done.
aiMasks: Documentation quality can make incomplete readiness look complete.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: AI-generated test or ticket artifacts create the illusion of completeness without operational closure.
exampleSignals: It is done for engineering.
We are basically done.

entries.title: Reporting looks healthier than delivery feels
entries.summary: Dashboards, status updates, and leadership narratives stay calm and positive while the teams doing the work feel far more fragility and risk.
entries.decisionHeuristic: When reporting and lived reality diverge consistently, the reporting system is becoming fiction.
entries.whatYouNotice: Status is green-ish, but delivery teams describe churn, risk, ambiguity, and weak confidence.
entries.whyItMatters: Leaders make decisions against a polished abstraction instead of operational truth.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: teams are temporarily more anxious than the underlying data justifies, and this is examined openly
leadingIndicators: teams use caveats that disappear in higher-level reporting
escalations feel socially expensive
leaders are surprised by issues that were visible locally
diagnosticQuestions: What gets lost as status moves upward?
Which risks are being softened or translated away?
Do teams believe escalation is safe and useful?
commonRootCauses: reporting structures that reward calm over truth
antiPatterns: polishing risk language for leadership consumption
using green status as an expected norm rather than a real signal
falseFriends: leaders just need simpler status views
aiAmplifies: AI can polish status summaries and make divergence between narrative and reality harder to notice.
aiMasks: Summaries become more articulate even when truth quality falls.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: AI-generated executive updates compress uncertainty into optimism-shaped prose.
exampleSignals: The slides say green, but nobody on the team believes green.
Leadership was surprised by something the team has worried about for weeks.

entries.title: Teams are measured on output, not outcome
entries.summary: Success is tracked through activity volume, ticket throughput, or artifact production more than through user, business, or system outcomes.
entries.decisionHeuristic: When the score rewards motion more than impact, motion will dominate.
entries.whatYouNotice: Teams optimize for visible delivery counts, not for whether the work improved the situation it was meant to change.
entries.whyItMatters: Output-oriented measurement often creates local optimization and synthetic velocity.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: output is being used as a temporary proxy in an area where outcome signals genuinely lag and this is acknowledged
leadingIndicators: throughput is celebrated more than effect
teams close lots of work but core pain remains
measurement discussions avoid user or operational reality
diagnosticQuestions: What real problem improved because of this work?
Would the team make different choices if measured on outcome?
What is easy to count that is crowding out what matters?
antiPatterns: mistaking utilization and throughput for impact
managing by volume because outcomes are harder to discuss
falseFriends: high output proves execution excellence
aiAmplifies: AI makes outputs cheaper to generate, which can worsen output-biased measurement rapidly.
aiMasks: High artifact volume can look like progress even when underlying complexity is unsolved.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: AI-generated tickets, docs, code, and summaries inflate visible output without equivalent outcome movement.
exampleSignals: We delivered 400 story points.
The question is how much we shipped, not what changed.

entries.title: Ownership and authority do not match
entries.summary: Teams or individuals are held accountable for outcomes they do not have enough control to influence.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Accountability without authority is usually deferred blame, not ownership.
entries.whatYouNotice: Owners need approval from several other parties to make the changes they are responsible for delivering.
entries.whyItMatters: This creates frustration, delays, and weak stewardship because responsibility and control are split.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: a temporary transition is underway and the mismatch is actively being resolved
leadingIndicators: owners escalate constantly
decision latency is high
people are blamed for issues they cannot directly change
diagnosticQuestions: What decisions can the owner make without permission?
Who controls the actual bottlenecks?
Where is accountability being assigned symbolically?
antiPatterns: solving mismatch with better communication alone
assigning owners without changing decision rights
falseFriends: clear accountability labels mean authority is clear too
aiAmplifies: AI can make teams appear more self-sufficient on paper while approval bottlenecks remain unchanged.
aiMasks: Better reporting does not fix missing authority.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Teams are expected to adopt AI quickly but lack authority over tools, policies, or supporting workflows.
exampleSignals: The team owns the service, but cannot change the deployment path.
They are accountable for reliability but do not control key dependencies.

entries.title: Platform work has no users, only sponsors
entries.summary: Internal platform or foundation work is funded and praised politically, but lacks real, visible, engaged adopters.
entries.decisionHeuristic: A platform without real users is usually an organizational aspiration, not a product.
entries.whatYouNotice: The work is justified upward, but actual consumer pull, adoption pain, or real product fit remains vague.
entries.whyItMatters: Internal systems become elegant artifacts without operational leverage.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: the platform is pre-launch but has explicit waiting adopters and adoption milestones
leadingIndicators: success is reported through capabilities built, not adoption gained
consumer feedback is sparse
roadmap is driven more by leadership narrative than user need
diagnosticQuestions: Who are the active users?
What real pain is being removed today?
How is adoption measured?
antiPatterns: measuring platform success by features delivered
building reusability in search of demand
falseFriends: leadership support means market fit is internalized
aiAmplifies: AI makes internal tooling and platform surfaces easier to build, which increases the risk of building them before demand is real.
aiMasks: AI-generated prototypes look polished enough to attract sponsor excitement without consumer validation.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: AI platform initiatives are celebrated before a durable internal use case exists.
exampleSignals: Leadership is excited, but no team is waiting to adopt it.
We built the platform; adoption will come later.

entries.title: Risks are acknowledged but never priced into plans
entries.summary: People discuss risks intelligently, but schedules, scope, staffing, and commitments do not change to reflect them.
entries.decisionHeuristic: A risk that does not affect the plan is usually only being narrated, not managed.
entries.whatYouNotice: Risks appear in logs, updates, and caveats, but commitments remain unchanged as if those risks were free.
entries.whyItMatters: The organization performs awareness without absorbing consequence.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: a risk is low probability and a deliberate conscious decision is documented
leadingIndicators: risks are repeated every update
plans stay constant despite worsening evidence
mitigations are shallow or symbolic
diagnosticQuestions: What changed in the plan because of this risk?
What budget, time, or scope was reserved for it?
Who accepted the cost if the risk lands?
antiPatterns: treating risk discussion as mitigation
keeping risk registers detached from resource planning
falseFriends: we have identified the risk, so we are managing it
aiAmplifies: AI can generate higher-quality risk summaries that still do nothing to force real trade-offs.
aiMasks: More articulate risk reporting can create false comfort.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: AI-related risks are documented elegantly while rollout pace and controls remain unchanged.
exampleSignals: Yes, that is a major risk, but the timeline is unchanged.
We are tracking the risk closely.

entries.title: Governance exists mainly as ceremony
entries.summary: Governance structures consume time and artifacts but have weak effect on actual risk, quality, or decision quality.
entries.decisionHeuristic: If governance adds friction without changing outcomes, it is drifting toward ceremony.
entries.whatYouNotice: Reviews, forums, sign-offs, and process steps are performed because they are expected, not because they meaningfully improve choices.
entries.whyItMatters: Ceremonial governance reduces trust, slows work, and encourages workarounds.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: lightweight recurring reviews are intentionally maintaining shared visibility and do change behavior
leadingIndicators: same approvals recur with low information value
teams prepare artifacts mainly to get through the process
decisions are often made before the governance step
diagnosticQuestions: What concrete decision quality or risk reduction comes from this forum?
Would outcomes worsen measurably if this step disappeared?
Who trusts this process and why?
antiPatterns: adding more sign-offs after every incident
judging governance success by participation rather than outcomes
falseFriends: high attendance means the governance is valuable
aiAmplifies: AI can make governance artifacts faster to generate, increasing ceremonial throughput without improving substance.
aiMasks: Well-formatted AI-generated evidence can make weak governance look rigorous.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: AI governance produces many forms and guidelines, but local practice remains unchanged.
exampleSignals: We need the document because the board expects it.
The review happened, but nobody changed anything.

entries.title: Nobody can say what the company will stop doing
entries.summary: Priorities accumulate, but explicit stopping decisions are rare or absent.
entries.decisionHeuristic: An organization that only adds priorities is usually hiding its real trade-offs in people’s capacity and burnout.
entries.whatYouNotice: New initiatives, expectations, and standards appear, but old ones rarely leave formally.
entries.whyItMatters: Strategic focus degrades, teams overcommit, and every new priority becomes somebody’s invisible burden.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: the organization is in a short-term expansion phase and has explicitly funded the extra load
leadingIndicators: teams inherit more priorities than capacity
everything remains important quarter after quarter
sunset conversations rarely happen
diagnosticQuestions: What work was intentionally stopped this quarter?
What priorities displaced which other priorities?
Where is the cost of not stopping absorbed?
commonRootCauses: political sponsorship of too many initiatives
antiPatterns: adding 'focus' language without removing commitments
treating stopping as failure rather than portfolio hygiene
falseFriends: we can just work smarter across all priorities
aiAmplifies: AI can make expansion look more manageable by increasing artifact and planning throughput without increasing actual human capacity.
aiMasks: Better summaries and plans can hide strategic overcommitment.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: AI adoption becomes one more priority layer without retiring anything else.
exampleSignals: We need to do all of this.
Nothing is coming off the list.

entries.title: More output, less certainty
entries.summary: The volume of produced artifacts increases, but confidence in correctness, design quality, or shared understanding does not keep pace.
entries.decisionHeuristic: If output rises faster than certainty, the system is probably borrowing confidence from appearance.
entries.whatYouNotice: More code, docs, tickets, or analyses appear quickly, but reviewers, owners, or users feel less sure what is actually trustworthy.
entries.whyItMatters: This is one of the clearest signals of synthetic velocity.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: the team is in a bounded experimentation phase and is explicitly studying this gap
leadingIndicators: authors hedge more despite faster production
reviewers rely on green checks over understanding
teams reopen work more often after 'faster delivery'
diagnosticQuestions: What confidence mechanism kept pace with the output increase?
Who actually trusts the new artifacts?
What got faster without getting safer?
antiPatterns: celebrating throughput without confidence evidence
assuming more generated artifacts equals more solved work
falseFriends: we are more productive because we produced more
aiAmplifies: This is the core AI-amplified pattern itself.
aiMasks: High polish and volume obscure falling confidence.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: AI-generated artifacts create a productivity narrative while shared certainty actually declines.
exampleSignals: We shipped much more, but I trust it less.
The deck looks great, but I do not know if the system is healthier.

entries.title: AI-generated artifacts are trusted more than source material
entries.summary: Summaries, synthesized docs, or generated analyses start becoming the operational truth instead of pointers back to real sources.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Generated synthesis should accelerate source access, not replace it in high-stakes contexts.
entries.whatYouNotice: Teams quote the summary rather than checking the doc, the code, the log, or the transcript it came from.
entries.whyItMatters: Compression improves convenience while increasing the chance of subtle but important distortion.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: the source is trivial and the summary is purely convenience for low-risk use
leadingIndicators: people cannot point to source evidence behind important claims
generated summaries get reused without verification
nuance disappears from decisions
diagnosticQuestions: What is the source of this claim?
Was the underlying material checked by a human?
Would the conclusion change if we read the source directly?
antiPatterns: replacing primary-source review with AI digest review
using summaries in place of logs, code, or data inspection
falseFriends: the summary is probably accurate enough
aiAmplifies: This red flag is directly AI-amplified.
aiMasks: High-quality prose can make weak synthesis feel authoritative.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: AI-generated architecture or incident summaries become the shared memory instead of source-linked interpretation.
exampleSignals: The summary said that was already decided.
I did not read the source doc; the AI recap was enough.

entries.title: Prompt changes replace system thinking
entries.summary: Teams keep tuning prompts when the real problem is workflow design, source quality, evaluation, or tool structure.
entries.decisionHeuristic: If prompt editing is your main response to repeated failure, you may be solving the wrong layer.
entries.whatYouNotice: Behavior issues trigger more prompt iterations, while data, retrieval, tool use, and workflow design remain weak.
entries.whyItMatters: Prompt iteration can create local improvements while system quality remains fundamentally unstable.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: the task is genuinely small and prompt tuning is the right local control surface
leadingIndicators: prompts keep getting longer
failure categories recur despite prompt edits
workflow and tool design stays static
diagnosticQuestions: Is this a prompt problem or a system problem?
What evidence says the prompt is the right lever?
What does the model lack besides wording?
antiPatterns: solving every failure with another paragraph in the prompt
avoiding instrumentation by over-editing wording
falseFriends: a better prompt is always the fastest correct fix
aiAmplifies: This red flag is AI-native.
aiMasks: Prompt tweaks can create enough visible improvement to delay deeper fixes.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Teams grow elaborate system prompts instead of repairing retrieval, tool use, or evaluation design.
exampleSignals: Let us just add one more instruction.
The prompt is now 3 pages long, but quality is still unstable.

entries.title: Benchmarks are discussed more than real user outcomes
entries.summary: Teams spend more time on benchmark scores and synthetic eval wins than on whether the system helps real users in real tasks.
entries.decisionHeuristic: Benchmarks are useful only if they stay subordinate to task reality.
entries.whatYouNotice: Model or system discussions center on scores, leaderboards, or eval deltas more than real-world workflow quality.
entries.whyItMatters: Proxy success can become a substitute for product truth.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: the team is still pre-launch and explicitly using benchmarks as temporary screening proxies
leadingIndicators: user outcomes are harder to state than eval scores
decisions are made from benchmark deltas alone
real-world incidents do not change benchmark enthusiasm
diagnosticQuestions: What user task got better?
Which benchmark result actually predicts user value here?
Where do our benchmarks diverge from production?
antiPatterns: shipping because the benchmark improved
tuning to evaluation sets rather than user tasks
falseFriends: if the benchmark is strong, the product must be strong
aiAmplifies: AI product culture is especially vulnerable to proxy overfocus.
aiMasks: Quantitative eval gains can feel more solid than messy real-world evidence.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: The team argues about eval deltas while users still fail at the job to be done.
exampleSignals: The benchmark is up 4 points, so we are clearly better.
We do not yet have real user data, but the evals look strong.

entries.title: Humans in the loop are rubber stamps
entries.summary: Human review exists in the workflow, but the review step does not preserve meaningful judgment.
entries.decisionHeuristic: A human checkpoint only matters if it still changes outcomes.
entries.whatYouNotice: Reviewers approve quickly, trust the system by default, or lack enough context and time to challenge outputs meaningfully.
entries.whyItMatters: This creates the appearance of safety without the actual benefit of human oversight.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: the review step is intentionally lightweight because the risk is genuinely low
leadingIndicators: approval rates are near-total
review times are extremely short
reviewers cannot explain what they are meant to catch
diagnosticQuestions: What decisions do reviewers meaningfully alter?
Do reviewers have the context to challenge the output?
Is the process optimized for judgment or throughput?
antiPatterns: adding humans as a comfort layer without redesigning the task
measuring human review mainly by speed
falseFriends: a human touched it, so it is safe
aiAmplifies: AI systems invite this red flag whenever autonomy pressure rises faster than review design quality.
aiMasks: Human presence in the workflow reassures stakeholders even when the review is ineffective.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Reviewers become final-click operators for model outputs they do not deeply inspect.
exampleSignals: Humans are still in the loop, so it is safe.
Reviewers approve almost everything anyway.

entries.title: RAG uses sources nobody actually trusts
entries.summary: Retrieval-based systems cite or use sources that are stale, low quality, politically loaded, or not actually treated as authoritative by humans.
entries.decisionHeuristic: A grounded answer is only as trustworthy as the sources it grounds to.
entries.whatYouNotice: The system cites documents that people would not use as authoritative in serious work.
entries.whyItMatters: RAG can look responsible while still grounding to bad truth.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: the system is explicitly exploratory and not treated as authoritative
leadingIndicators: users say the citations are technically relevant but not reliable
important docs and casual notes are treated similarly
stale sources keep appearing
diagnosticQuestions: Would a human trust this source for a real decision?
What signals determine source authority?
Do we distinguish between accessible and authoritative?
commonRootCauses: ranking based on recall over trust
likelyConsequences: erosion of trust in the assistant
antiPatterns: assuming citation equals correctness
indexing everything because more data feels safer
falseFriends: it cited sources, so it is grounded
the source is available, so it is trustworthy
aiAmplifies: This is a core AI-specific red flag.
aiMasks: Citations make low-trust sources feel authoritative.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: The assistant becomes confidently wrong with references.
exampleSignals: It cited a document nobody would trust in an actual review.
The source exists, but it is outdated and unofficial.

entries.title: Model drift is noticed informally, not measured
entries.summary: People sense that model or system behavior changed, but the organization lacks reliable measurement, alerts, or structured comparison.
entries.decisionHeuristic: If drift is discovered by vibes first, monitoring is underpowered.
entries.whatYouNotice: Users say the system feels different or worse, but the team cannot quantify when, how, or why behavior shifted.
entries.whyItMatters: Unmeasured drift weakens trust and delays corrective action.
notNecessarilyAProblemWhen: a temporary exploratory prototype is explicitly not treated as stable
leadingIndicators: user anecdotes drive concern before metrics do
qualitative complaints recur without quantified evidence
provider or prompt changes are poorly tracked
diagnosticQuestions: What baseline behavior are we comparing against?
What changed in prompt, model, retrieval, or tooling?
Which task slices are most vulnerable to drift?
antiPatterns: relying on anecdote without building drift measurement
treating provider updates as invisible infrastructure changes
falseFriends: we would know if the model meaningfully changed
aiAmplifies: This is an AI-specific operating risk.
aiMasks: General system success can hide slice-specific degradation until users complain loudly enough.
entries.aiSpecificNotes: Provider or pipeline changes alter behavior while the team lacks a living baseline.
exampleSignals: It feels worse lately, but we do not know why.
Users noticed the change before our monitoring did.
